Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit unconventional. I'd dedicate about 10 hours a week, mostly in the evenings after work and other responsibilities. I'm a read-write learner, so I'd focus heavily on taking detailed notes from the course materials and then rewriting them in my own words. I didn't follow a strict schedule, but I made sure to log in regularly and keep track of what needed to be done, even if my actual class attendance was pretty low.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying a little bit each day, but other weeks everything would pile up and I'd have to cram. I've learned to be flexible because life doesn't always cooperate with a perfect schedule. Deadlines were usually what determined when I'd put in the most intense effort.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements, looking at upcoming deadlines, or wanting to participate in discussions. I actually enjoyed the discussion forums quite a bit—they helped me feel connected to the course even when I couldn't attend live sessions. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch more video content because I found myself getting really absorbed in the lectures. The notification emails also prompted me to check in regularly.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became almost automatic for me. I'd check it every morning with my coffee, just like checking email or the news. It happened naturally because I wanted to stay on top of things despite my low attendance. The discussion posts became something I looked forward to because interacting with peers made the course feel less isolating.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly valuable—I watched over 150 minutes of content because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the material at my own pace. The discussion forums were also crucial for me because they let me process ideas by writing them out and getting feedback from classmates. Reading materials that I could download and annotate were my bread and butter since that matches how I learn best.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't complete all the assignments—my completion rate wasn't great even though when I did submit work, I scored really well. Some of the interactive activities felt redundant when I'd already understood the concept through reading and note-taking. I tended to skip anything that felt like busywork or didn't directly connect to what I needed to know for exams and major assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my understanding or my grade. If I walked away feeling like I'd learned something concrete or could apply it, that was valuable. I also valued activities that let me engage deeply with the material in my preferred way—reading and writing. The CBT sessions I attended also helped me recognize when I was being too hard on myself about what was 'worth it.'
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which sometimes made them more enjoyable but also easier to skip when time got tight. Required activities had this weight to them that made me prioritize them even when I was tired or stressed. But honestly, some of the optional discussion posts were more engaging than required ones because I chose topics I genuinely cared about.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs, lecture slides, and any reading materials, then print them out or save them to my tablet. I'd spend hours with my handwritten notes, rewriting and reorganizing information. I also used external resources like YouTube videos and articles to supplement what the course provided. My best studying happened with a notebook and pen, away from the computer.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd download a lecture, watch it on my commute, and then spend the evening writing notes and making connections—none of that showed up online. I'd also discuss concepts with friends who were taking similar courses, which helped solidify my understanding. My exam score reflects learning that happened mostly off-platform.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd access a PDF or video once, download it, and then work with it for days or even weeks afterward. My approach was to gather materials during my LMS sessions and then process them offline where I could focus better. I found I retained more when I wasn't distracted by the computer interface and could just work with the content directly.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when multiple deadlines collided or when I felt overwhelmed by the volume of content in the online courses I was taking. My anxiety would spike a bit during those times. But I've developed pretty strong resilience—the CBT sessions really helped me manage those moments. I'd remind myself that struggling doesn't mean failing, and I'd break things into smaller pieces.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were probably my biggest challenge. I'm 23 and juggling a lot, so sometimes the LMS got less attention than it deserved. Fatigue was real too—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was more screen time. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely and try to figure things out through other resources first. But I always came back.
Q14: Honestly, yes. Sometimes I'd be scrolling through materials or watching videos but my mind was elsewhere—thinking about other stresses or just mentally exhausted. I'd realize I'd 'watched' 20 minutes of lecture without absorbing anything. Those moments taught me to recognize when I needed a break rather than pushing through unproductively. Quality of engagement mattered more than quantity.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments, which is why my completion rate was lower than I'd like. Sometimes it was because I felt anxious about not doing them perfectly, other times I genuinely ran out of time. The CBT sessions helped me understand this pattern—I'd avoid things that felt overwhelming, even when I knew they were important. I'm working on breaking tasks into smaller, less intimidating pieces.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. I'd log in more frequently, watch more videos, and participate more in discussions when I knew something was coming up. It's not ideal, but that pressure helped me focus. Without deadlines, I think I'd spread my effort too thin across all my courses.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback on assignments—and my scores were usually high when I submitted—it motivated me to engage more. It validated that my study approach was working even if it looked unconventional. Constructive criticism helped me adjust my focus. The lack of immediate feedback on some activities made them feel less urgent though.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I needed, but it also required a lot of self-discipline. Having 13 online courses meant I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. The discussion forums being consistently available helped me participate on my own schedule. The clear organization of materials made it easier to find what I needed during my intensive study sessions.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made engagement much easier—I could learn when my energy was highest. The discussion forums being active helped too because I could engage with peers asynchronously. What made it harder was when too many things were due at once, or when the connection between activities and assessments wasn't clear. I needed to see the purpose behind what I was doing.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to translate that into completed work. Maybe they're perfectionists who can't submit until something feels 'ready,' or they're dealing with external pressures that eat into their time. High activity shows effort and interest—the gap with submissions might be about confidence, time management, or overwhelming circumstances.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My attendance was very low and my assignment completion wasn't great, but I passed with a decent exam score and high assignment quality when I did submit. I studied extensively offline, engaged deeply with downloaded materials, and participated meaningfully in discussions. The LMS doesn't capture studying that happens with printed notes and handwritten summaries.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often you accessed materials, participation in discussions, and submission patterns. They're good at tracking digital footprints. But they miss the depth of engagement—whether someone was truly absorbing content or just clicking through. They can't see offline studying, emotional investment, or the quality of understanding developed. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's about curiosity, asking questions, making connections to other things I know, and feeling motivated to learn more. For me, engagement looks like filling notebooks with notes, participating in discussions because I want to hear others' perspectives, and thinking about course concepts outside of study time. It's an active mental and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with printed materials, rewriting notes, or discussing concepts with friends. It wouldn't capture the personal growth I experienced through the CBT sessions and how that affected my approach to learning. It can't measure my resilience—how I bounced back from difficult moments—or the genuine happiness I felt when concepts clicked. The data shows behavior, but my engagement was much deeper than clicks and timestamps.
